statement: Alþýðusamband Íslands (ASÍ, Icelandic Confederation of Labour) has maintained a carefully balanced position on EU membership. The ASÍ executive board voted in 2024 to support the referendum process without endorsing membership, by a margin of 7–4 (with the minority favouring explicit support). ASÍ's primary concerns are: (1) protection of Iceland's collective bargaining model, which achieves ~100% coverage without statutory intervention; (2) wage levels — average wages in Iceland are 15–20% above the EU average in PPP terms; (3) social security coordination, where EEA implementation gaps disadvantage Icelandic workers abroad. ASÍ has noted that the EU Minimum Wage Directive explicitly accommodates the Nordic model (countries with >80% collective agreement coverage are exempt from statutory minimum wage requirements).
source_name: ASÍ — Executive board minutes and public statements; ASÍ annual congress 2024
caveats: ASÍ is a federation of individual unions with varying views. Some constituent unions (particularly in tourism and services) are more favourable to EU membership, while manufacturing and public sector unions are more sceptical. The 7–4 board vote reveals the depth of internal division.

related_entries.statement: Samtök atvinnulífsins (SA, Confederation of Icelandic Enterprise) has broadly supported EU membership, arguing it would reduce trade barriers, lower the cost of capital, and provide a more stable regulatory environment. Alþýðusamband Íslands (ASÍ, Icelandic Confederation of Labour) has been more cautious, supporting the referendum process but not formally endorsing membership. ASÍ's concerns centre on whether EU labour market rules would weaken the Icelandic collective bargaining model, which achieves high union density (~90%) and strong wage outcomes.

Iceland's labour market regulation differs from the EU norm in several key respects. Union density is approximately 90% (vs EU average ~23%), and wages are set primarily through centralised collective agreements between ASÍ and SA rather than through legislation or sectoral bargaining. There is no statutory minimum wage — the collective agreement minimum functions as the de facto floor. Iceland has already transposed most EU employment directives through the EEA, including working time, equal treatment, and posted workers rules. Key areas where EU membership could bring additional regulation include works councils (European Works Council Directive has limited EEA application), co-determination, and the proposed EU minimum wage directive.

Iceland has the highest trade union density in the OECD at approximately 90%, far exceeding all EU member states. By comparison, union density is approximately 67% in Denmark, 65% in Sweden, 59% in Finland, 50% in Norway, and only 23% on average across the EU-27. This extraordinary level of unionisation is sustained by Iceland's system of automatic extension of collective agreements, where agreements negotiated between union federations and employer associations are legally binding on all workers in the relevant sector, regardless of union membership. EU membership could introduce additional frameworks (European Works Councils, European Company Statutes) but is unlikely to threaten Iceland's collective bargaining model, as the EU Minimum Wage Directive explicitly protects high-coverage systems.
